content.content:
In the last challenge, you found out how to avoid obstacles on the map and how to pick up any artefacts you encountered. In this challenge you are going to try to pick up ten artefacts and examine what type of artefact you
have found.

leftColumn.leftColumn:
Your avatar has been wandering the map aimlessly
up to now. In this challenge, you are trying to pick
up ten artefacts, so it is important that your avatar
goes the right way!

Start with this code on the right.

Rather than set a specific direction for your avatar,
you are going to change your code so that the
avatar favours moving in a particular direction.

If, for example, you notice that there are lots of
artefacts North-East of you, you are going to head
that way.

rightColumn.rightColumn:
There are lots of way to achieve this, one is to generate a larger range of
random numbers so that you can favour some directions. For example, if
you generate 6 random numbers, you can favour going North or East like
this code on the left.

Change your program so that you have more control over the direction your
avatar heads in.

content.content:
__Extension__: You could make this more flexible by having a list of directions in order of preference and then choosing from there. 
E.g. `favoured = [direction.SOUTH, direction.WEST, direction.NORTH, direction.EAST]`

Now you have more control over your avatar’s direction, can you pick up ten artefacts? You can look in your backpack using this command:

```python
my_backpack = avatar_state.backpack
print("I have", len(my_backpack), "things in my backpack.")
```

Try this.

leftColumn.leftColumn:
__IMPORTANT__

Kurono uses subroutines. If you create a variable inside a subroutine, it is local so other subroutines cannot access it directly. Its value is also reset every time you call the subroutine. This code on the right will not work! It will always print "There are 1 things in my backpack", no matter how many artefacts you have picked up.

rightColumn.rightColumn:
```python
count = 0

current_cell = world_state.get_cell(
    avatar_state.location
)

if current_cell.has_artefact():
    # There is an artefact here, pick it up
    count = count + 1 # Local variable
    print(
        "I have", count, "things in my backpack"
    )
    action = PickupAction()
else:
    action = MoveAction(direction.NORTH)

return action
```

leftColumn.leftColumn: This code lets you look inside your backpack:

rightColumn.rightColumn:
```python
my_backpack = avatar_state.backpack
count = len(my_backpack)
print("I have", count, "things in my backpack.")
```

content.content: Add some code that outputs how many items you have in your backpack and prints out "I have ten artefacts, I win!" when you have picked up ten.

You might have noticed that there are different types of artefacts. Later on, you will need to check their type before picking them. For now, just make sure you know what you have.

In this challenge, there are only red and blue artefacts. You can find out what you have in your backpack by looking at the `artefact.type` for an item in your backpack.

This code outputs the artefact type of the first artefact in your backpack.

__NB:__ This code will crash if your backpack is empty!

```python
artefact = my_backpack[0]
print("The first one is", artefact.type)
```

__Challenge part 1__: Expand your program so that it outputs the types of all artefacts in your backpack when you have picked up ten.

__Challenge part 2__: The artefacts on this level are red or blue only. Expand your program so that it outputs a summary of what you have in your backpack. 
E.g. *I have 6 red and 4 blue artefacts.*

Well done! You are really getting to grips with controlling your avatar now. You have found out how to control direction, how to examine your backpack and how to find out about the artefacts you are holding.
